coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical/visual dual-readout aptasensor for Ochratoxin A detection integrated into a miniaturized paper-based analytical device
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaobo
Zhi, Hui
Zhu, Mingzhen
Wang, Fengya
Meng, Hu
Feng, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Development of portable, sensitive and reliable devices for Ochratoxin A (OTA) detection is highly demanded, especially for resource-limited regions. Herein, a novel paper-based analytical device (PAD) is designed through wax printing and screen-printed technologies, which integrates sample flowing, electrode modification, cleaning and electrochemical (EC)/colorimetric signal output. To greatly enhance the detection sensitivity, we synthesized a chitosan functionalized MoS2–Au@Pt (Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt) via electrostatic self-assembly, and used it to immobilize the label aptamer (apta2) for signal regulation and amplification. Concretely, with the addition of analytes, the Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt-apta2 could be combined on the sensing interface by specific biorecognition and catalyzed reduction of H2O2, resulting in a remarkable EC response. Meanwhile, the released hydroxyl radicals (·OH) flowed to the visualization zone and promoted the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine for colorimetric detection. Consequently, the dual-mode PAD achieved acceptable prediction and accurate analysis in the range of 0.1–200 ng mL−1 and 1 × 10−4-200 ng mL−1 by matching the visual and EC signal intensity, respectively. Compared with traditional single-mode sensor for OTA, the proposed dual-mode aptasensor featuring independent signal conversion and readout, not only avoided the false-positive signal associated with detection condition and operation, but also enlarged the detection ranges and improved the sensitivity. Furthermore, the consistency of EC/colorimetric assay was validated in real OTA samples. Overall, this work provided a portable, cost-effective, sensitive and visualized aptasensor platform, which could be extended to various other mycotoxins in the field of food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ochratoxin A
Paper-based analytical device
Electrochemical/colorimetric signal
Aptasensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and assay of dual-mode PAD
Morphology and structure characterization of the nanomaterials
Evaluation of electroactive surface area of MnO2–Au
Comparison of POD-like activity with different signal amplification labels
Electrochemical characterization of the stepwise fabricated dual-mode PAD
Feasibility study of the dual-mode sensing system
Analytical performance of the dual-mode OTA PAD aptsensor
Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the dual-mode PAD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of MnO2 NF (A) and MnO2–Au (B). (C) XRD patterns of MnO2 NF and MnO2–Au. (D) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm of the synthesized MnO2 NF and MnO2–Au.

TEM images of MoS2 NS (A), Au@Pt NPs (B) and Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt (D). (C) HRTEM images of Au@Pt NPs. (E) XRD patterns of MoS2 NS and Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt. (F) Zeta potential of MoS2 NS (a), Ch-MoS2 NS (b), Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt (c), and Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt-apta2 (d).

CV curves of bare WE (A) and MnO2–Au/WE (B) at different scan rates range from 10 to 200 mV s−1 in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– and the linear relationship between the reduction peak current I
p and v
1/2 . (C) Photographs and UV–visible absorption spectra of different systems: (a) TMB, (b) TMB + H2O2, (c) TMB + H2O2+Ch-MoS2 NS, (d) TMB + H2O2+Ch-MoS2-Au, and (e) TMB + H2O2+Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt in PBS (pH 7.0). (D) Steady-state kinetic assays of Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt. The velocity (v) of the reaction was measured using Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt in 5 mL PBS (pH 7.0), where the concentration of TMB was 20 mM and the concentration of H2O2 varied from 0.3 to 2 mM. Inset: Double reciprocal plots of activity of Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt. (E) CV and (F) EIS of the with stepwise-modified aptsensor: (a) bare WE, (b) MnO2–Au/WE, (c) apta1/MnO2–Au/WE, (d) BSA/apta1/MnO2–Au/WE, (e) OTA/BSA/apta1/MnO2–Au/WE, and (f) Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt-apta2/OTA/BSA/apta1/MnO2–Au/WE in 0.1 M KCl electrolyte solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– as redox probe. The inset in (F) is Randles equivalent circuit for EIS.

(A) EC responses of the PAD incubated with different concentrations of OTA (a–h: 100 fg mL−1, 1 pg mL−1, 10 pg mL−1, 100 pg mL−1, 1 ng mL−1, 10 ng mL−1, 100 ng mL−1, and 200 ng mL−1) and calibration curve. (B) Colorimetric signal of the PAD incubated with different concentrations of OTA (a–e: 100 pg mL−1, 1 ng mL−1, 10 ng mL−1, 100 ng mL−1, and 200 ng mL−1) and calibration curve. (C) The EC selectivity towards 1 ng mL−1 OTA mix with 100 ng mL−1 interference including AFB1, ZEN, DON, and T-2. (D) The EC reproducibility for 1 pg mL−1, 1 ng mL−1, and 100 ng mL−1 OTA. Error bars = SD (n = 3).
Illustration of modification (A) and cleaning (B) process of the PAD, (C) fabrication procedures and sensing principle of EC/colorimetric PAD.
Comparison of the analytical performance with other reported techniques for OTA detection.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical/visual dual-readout aptasensor for Ochratoxin A detection integrated into a miniaturized paper-based analytical device

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Instrumentation and Analytical Chemistry, CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 457 Zhongshan Road, Dalian, 116023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Instrumentation and Analytical Chemistry
CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Instrumentation and Analytical Chemistry, CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 457 Zhongshan Road, Dalian, 116023, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of portable, sensitive and reliable devices for Ochratoxin A (OTA) detection is highly demanded, especially for resource-limited regions. Herein, a novel paper-based analytical device (PAD) is designed through wax printing and screen-printed technologies, which integrates sample flowing, electrode modification, cleaning and electrochemical (EC)/colorimetric signal output. To greatly enhance the detection sensitivity, we synthesized a chitosan functionalized MoS2–Au@Pt (Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt) via electrostatic self-assembly, and used it to immobilize the label aptamer (apta2) for signal regulation and amplification. Concretely, with the addition of analytes, the Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt-apta2 could be combined on the sensing interface by specific biorecognition and catalyzed reduction of H2O2, resulting in a remarkable EC response. Meanwhile, the released hydroxyl radicals (·OH) flowed to the visualization zone and promoted the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine for colorimetric detection. Consequently, the dual-mode PAD achieved acceptable prediction and accurate analysis in the range of 0.1–200 ng mL−1 and 1 × 10−4-200 ng mL−1 by matching the visual and EC signal intensity, respectively. Compared with traditional single-mode sensor for OTA, the proposed dual-mode aptasensor featuring independent signal conversion and readout, not only avoided the false-positive signal associated with detection condition and operation, but also enlarged the detection ranges and improved the sensitivity. Furthermore, the consistency of EC/colorimetric assay was validated in real OTA samples. Overall, this work provided a portable, cost-effective, sensitive and visualized aptasensor platform, which could be extended to various other mycotoxins in the field of food safety.

ce:section.ce:para: Sensitive and precise detection of food contaminants plays a crucial role in public food safety. Ochratoxin A (OTA), as the most common and carcinogenic contaminants in agricultural products, is produced by Aspergillus and Penicillium fungal species (Giray et al., 2009). Exposure to OTA can provoke severe carcinogenicity and nephrotoxicity even at quite low concentrations (Pagkali et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2016). Furthermore, OTA is difficult to degrade in storage and production due to its high chemical and thermal stability. Currently, the gold-standard OTA detection methods mainly include thin layer chromatography (TLC), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), etc (Mejri-Omrani et al., 2016; Teixeira et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2017). Although these methods afford quantitative detection of OTA, they generally require skilled staff, sophisticated operation, long analysis time, and expensive equipment, which restrict their practical applicability for fast and on-site assay (Suea-Ngam et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017a). Therefore, it is desirable to develop a rapid and portable point-of-care testing (POCT) strategy for OTA, to lower the health and economic risks, particularly in developing countries and region with resource-constrained.

Recently, as most prominent techniques, aptasensors have attracted intensive attention in the field of diseases diagnostics, environmental monitoring and food industry (Liu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018b). Aptamers are single-stranded oligonucleotides, which can specifically bind with their corresponding target with high affinity. Besides, aptamer possesses several advantages such as ease of chemical functionalization, high stability, and low cost with little batch-to-batch variation (Rivas et al., 2015; Xing et al., 2020). At present, several pioneered works have been reported. For instance, Zhu et al. developed a sensitive electrochemical (EC) OTA aptasensor based on peroxidase (POD) -like activity of the g-C3N4 nanosheet and its high affinity toward aptamer (Zhu et al., 2018). He et al. presented a colorimetric method based on MnO2 nanosheets and alkaline phosphatase to enable naked-eye readout of OTA (Tian et al., 2019). Unfortunately, these analytical methods only provided single modality assay, and may suffer from the issue of high background signal and false-positive (Campu et al., 2018; Jiao et al., 2019). Accordingly, dual-modal aptasensors are a trend-setting strategy because they combine the advantages of EC with colorimetric methods, achieving high sensitivity, reliability and simple visualization detection.

The crucial issue is to design nanomaterial-based mimic enzymes as label to attach the aptamer for signal regulation and amplification. Appropriate signal label is crucial to effectively transform biorecognition events between the aptamer and the target analyte into a measurable electrical and colorimetric signal (Chen et al., 2018a; Ming et al., 2019). Compared with traditional enzymes, the nanostructured signal label provides the increased sensing interfaces for improving the capture efficiency of target, as well as the enhanced catalytic activity for corresponding reaction, endowing forth higher stability and sensitivity (Fan et al., 2018; Iglesias-Mayor et al., 2020). Diverse molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) based nanozymes have been demonstrated to possess intrinsic POD-like activity (Phan Thi Kim et al., 2014; Wei and Wang, 2013). Specifically, the coexistence of Mo4+ and Mo6+ endows MoS2 to become a reversible redox electron mediator to facilitate electron transfer (Chen et al., 2018b; Han et al., 2020). Inspired by this, bimetallic Au@Pt nanoparticles (NPs) were assembled on the chitosan functionalized MoS2 nanosheets (Ch-MoS2 NS) surface via electrostatic interaction, which was beneficial to expose plenty of catalytic active sites and reduce the aggregation of NPs (Xia et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2018). Based on the synergic effect of unique raspberry-like morphology of Au@Pt NPs and redox activity from abundant Mo centers, the prepared composite (Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt) displayed excellent catalytic activity. Thus, we believed that Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt could be utilized as a novel mimetic enzyme in dual-mode sensing system for signal amplification.

Herein, we developed an electrochemical/visual dual-readout aptasensor for the OTA detection integrated into a miniaturized paper-based analytical (PAD), on basis of aptamer biorecognition and Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt signal amplification. The folding and multilayered structure of PAD was engineered via wax printing and screen printing technologies, and integrated multiple functions including liquid flowing, electrode modification, cleaning and signal output The PAD platform showed remarkable advantages such as less reagent consumption, simpler operation and lower cost compared with traditional analytical methods (Zhang et al., 2018a). As illustrated in Scheme. 1 A, Au NPs supported MnO2 nanoflower (MnO2–Au) was first used to anchor capture aptamer (apta1) through Au–S bond and provided the increased electroactive surface area (An et al., 2019). With the addition of OTA, the sandwich complex (Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt-apta2/OTA/apta1) was formed on the sensing interface and effectively catalyzed H2O2 to generate the abundant hydroxyl radical (·OH), resulting in electrochemical signal enhancement. Meanwhile, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was oxidized by the released ·OH and produced large amount of colored TMB-ox (Chen et al., 2018a; Lu et al., 2020a). By matching the visual and electrochemical signal with the OTA concentration, acceptable prediction and accurate detection could be achieved. Excitingly, the designed dual-mode PAD with independent signal readout could give a complementation for OTA detection, and minimize error associated with detection condition and operation. The study provided an alternative POCT tool for sensitive, simple and visual detection of OTA, which hold great potential to other analytes in food analysis.

OTA, aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), zearalenone (ZEN), deoxynivalenol (DON) and T-2 toxin were purchased from Pribolab Technology Co., Ltd (Singapore). OTA apta1 and apta2: 5′–SH–(CH2)6-GATCGGGTGTGGGTGGCGTAAAGGGAGCATCGGACA-3′ were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The other details are provided in Supplementary Material.

Inspired by the fascinating advantages of the PAD, a folding origami device integrated electrochemical and colorimetric sensing system with multilayered structure was designed. Firstly, the unique device patterns were created using Adobe Illustrator CC and printed on Whatman grade 1 chromatography paper using the wax printer. Then the printed paper was placed in an oven at 200 °C for 3 min to create a wax barrier. As shown in Fig. S1A, Layer I contained a circular zone (3.0 mm in diameter) for visualization detection. Layer II consisted of two circular regions (4.0 and 6.0 mm in diameter), in which the larger circular pattern was utilized as for EC detection reservoir, and the other one was employed to gather released ·OH and connect with visualization zone via a rectangular hydrophilic channel (10 mm × 2.0 mm). Next, carbon working electrode (WE), carbon counter electrode (CE) and Ag/AgCl reference electrode (RE) were screen-printed on the hydrophilic pattern of Layer III and V to realize sensitive electrochemical determination (Fig. S1B). It is worth noting that the hollow rectangular zone of Layer III was employed to connect the auxiliary Layer IV to achieve modification and cleaning process (Scheme. 1A and B). The Layer IV consisted of the marked line, hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity rectangular area. Specifically, when the red line was aligned with the top of Layer IV, the modified solution was restored on the WE owing to overlaps of wax barrier and hollow zone, and prevented RE and CE from contaminating. Through slipping Layer IV to switch the state of hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity, the cleaning step was implemented to make physically adsorption flow from WE to hydrophilicity area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and assay of dual-mode PAD
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of the aptasensor was shown in Scheme. 1C. Briefly, 6 μL MnO2–Au suspension was coated on the surface of WE to improve surface area and the conductivity of sensing platform. After that, 6 μL apta1 (10 μg mL−1) was introduced and incubated at 37 °C for 40 min to obtain apta1 modified MnO2–Au/WE through the bonding between Au and –SH. The modified electrode was washed and incubated with 3 μL bovine serum albumin (BSA) solution (1%) for 40 min to block nonspecific binding sites (Chen et al., 2019). After different concentrations of OTA solution was added, 6 μL Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt-apta2 was conjugated on the sensing interface to obtain “apta1-OTA-apta2” sandwich formation due to the specific recognition between aptamer and OTA. To remove nonspecific binding molecules, the resulting sensing interfaces were rinsed with phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) after each modification procedure. Finally, the specific folded PAD was assembled with two resin plates and tightened with foldback clips to minimize deformation of PAD and fluid leakage between layers (Fig. S1C). The successfully fabricated PAD was connected to the electrochemical workstation with alligator clips (Fig. S1D).

As for accurate quantification of OTA, 120 μL PBS (pH 7.0, 5 mM H2O2) was cast into electrochemical detection reservoir. The electrochemical responses were measured by cyclic voltammetry (CV) from −0.8 V to +0.2 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. And the peak current signal produced by H2O2 reduction at around −0.44 V was recorded. Meanwhile, the electrolyte solution flowed through visualization detection zone driven by capillary forces, and initiated the chromogenic reaction with preloaded 10 μL TMB (20 mM). Ultimately, the color change images were recorded 3 min later using a scanner and its gray intensity was analyzed by applying ImageJ software. Featuring portability, simple operation, low cost and the minimized consumption of samples, the dual-mode PAD could present highly reliable and sensitive detection of OTA.

The POD-like activity of the as-prepared Ch–MoS2-Au@Pt nanocomposites was investigated by analyzing the current response of H2O2 reduction via CV. Besides, TMB (20 mM) as the chromogenic substrate was incubated with 10 μL different nanocatalysts (2 mg mL−1) for 3 min in 5 mL PBS (pH 7.0, 5 mM H2O2), followed by the generation of the blue TMB oxidation product. Then the characteristic absorbance peak at 650 nm was recorded with UV–vis. Finally, the Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) of Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt was calculated to support the good catalytic performance based on the Michaelis-Menten equation as follows: 1/v = (Km/v max) (1/[S]) + 1/v max, where ν is the initial reaction velocity, νmax is the maximal reaction velocity and [S] is the concentration of the substrate (Sun et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of the nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 A, the SEM images exhibited that the MnO2 nanoflower (MnO2 NF) possessed a flower-shaped morphology (~500 nm in diameter). After Au NPs was anchored on the surface of MnO2 NF via the reduction of AuCl4 −, no obvious difference in size and structure for MnO2 NF support were observed (Fig. 1B). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 1C) of pure MnO2 NF displayed three diffraction peaks at 12.3°, 37.4° and 65.5° corresponding to the (003), (012) and (110) planes of MnO2 (Meng et al., 2014). And for MnO2–Au, the diffraction peaks emerged at 38.5°, 44.6° and 77.8°, which were in agreement with the (111), (200) and (311) lattice planes of face-centered cubic Au, indicating the successful loading of Au NPs onto MnO2 NF (Sun et al., 2019).

Fig. 1D presented the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of MnO2 NF and MnO2–Au. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller surface area of the MnO2–Au was calculated to be 68.33 m2 g−1, which was higher than pure MnO2 NF (28.25 m2 g−1). It was supposed that the implantation of Au NPs on the surface of MnO2 NF increased the surface area (Lu et al., 2020b). The large surface area and specific porous structure endowed the MnO2–Au with abundant mass-transfer channels.

TEM images were used to characterize the morphologies of as-prepared MoS2 NS, Au@Pt NPs and Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt. As can be seen from Fig. 2 A, a typical two-dimensional thin-layer MoS2 NS was obtained. In Fig. 2B, Au@Pt NPs showed unique raspberry-like morphology with a particle size ranging from 50 to 60 nm. Meanwhile, based on the HRTEM image (Fig. 2C) of core and shell region from random Au@Pt NPs, the ordered lattice fringe with the d-spacing of 0.238 nm and 0.227 nm, which matched well with the planes of face-centered cubic Au and Pt, respectively (Guo et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2015). From Fig. 2D, the TEM images revealed Au@Pt NPs were successfully supported on the surface of MoS2 NS, and no obvious agglomeration was formed.

As shown in Fig. 2E, the characteristic diffraction peaks of MoS2 NS were located at 13.9°, 32.8° and 57.6°, corresponding to the (002), (100) and (110) planes of hexagonal phase MoS2 (JCPDS 37–1492), respectively (Kathiravan et al., 2019). After Ch modification, the characteristic peak (002) decreased to 9.5°, which indicated the interlayer spacing value of MoS2 increased because of successful intercalation of Ch (Saada and Bissessur, 2012). Besides, the strong diffraction peaks emerged at 38.5° and 46.3°, which can be corresponded to (111) of face-centered cubic Au (JCPDS 04–0784) and (200) of face-centered cubic Pt (JCPDS 04–0802), respectively (Su et al., 2016). The XRD date further indicated successful synthesis of Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt nanocomposites.

Zeta potential analysis was applied to evaluate the particle surface charge, stability of the nanocomposite suspensions and conjugation of Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt with apta2 (Thielbeer et al., 2011). As observed in Fig. 2F, the zeta potential value of the Ch-MoS2 NS was drastically increased to 43.5 mV. After the bond of negatively charged Au@Pt NPs on Ch-MoS2 NS, the value of the zeta potential decreased to −20.8 mV. As expected, the zeta potential value of Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt-apta2 shifted to a less negative value (−35.6 mV) due to negatively charged phosphate skeletons from apta2, demonstrating the labeling process was successful. Meanwhile, the absolute zeta potential values of different nanocomposite (MoS2 NS, Ch-MoS2 NS and Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt) were higher than 10 mV, corroborating the good stability of the NPs suspensions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of electroactive surface area of MnO2–Au
ce:section.ce:para:
The electroactive surface area of MnO2–Au could be significantly improved owing to the formation of nano-heterojunction between Au NPs and MnO2 NF. Then, the electroactive surface area of bare WE and MnO2–Au/WE can be approximately calculated via CV based on Randles–Sevcik equation (1) (Li et al., 2011):
(1)
I
p = 2.69 × 105AD1/2n3/2 v 1/2 c
where I
p is the peak current value, A is the electroactive surface area (cm2), n stands for the electron transfer number in the redox reaction (1 in this system), D = 6.70 × 10−6 cm2 s−1, which is the diffusion coefficient of [Fe(CN)6]3–/4–, c and v corresponds to the concentration of the redox probe and the CV scanning rate (V s−1), respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 A and B, the regression equations of bare WE and MnO2–Au/WE were obtained as I
p = 311.18 × v 1/2 + 9.54 and I
p = 460.37 × v 1/2 + 6.39, which correspond to the A values of 0.089 cm2 and 0.132 cm2, respectively. Indeed, the additional electrons in Au NPs contributed to the increased electron density of MnO2–Au, and thus remarkably increased the electroactive sensing interface. Furthermore, MnO2–Au/WE was scanned at the constant scan rate (50 mV s−1) for consecutive 10 cycles, the signal intensity had no significant changes (in Fig. S2), illustrating the good stability of the MnO2–Au. We presumed that MnO2–Au could act as an efficient sensing interface to facilitate electron transfer and enhance electrochemical signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of POD-like activity with different signal amplification labels
ce:section.ce:para:
The POD-like activity of different catalysts was investigated by CV. As shown in Fig. S3, a weak peak from the reduction of dissolved oxygen started at −0.65 V (a) without H2O2. And the curve remained almost constant in the presence of Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt (b), indicating that the composites do not exert any background noise. In the presence of H2O2, the WE towards the reduction of H2O2 was negligible (c). Interestingly, under the same conditions, Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt (f) shifted the reduction peak potential of the H2O2, from −0.54 V to −0.44 V, and a markedly increased current value could be observed compared to Ch-MoS2 NS (d) and Ch-MoS2-Au (e). The enhanced POD-like activity of Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt could be attributed to the synergistic effect among MoS2, Au and Pt, leading to a lower kinetic barrier of H2O2 decomposition and acceleration the free radical reaction. Furthermore, TMB, as a classical chromogenic substrate, was applied to evaluate the intrinsic POD-like property of the as-synthetized Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt via the following equation (2):
(2) TMBred + H2O2 → TMBox(blue)+H2O

From the absorption spectra at 650 nm and photographs of Fig. 3C, a strong blue color and high absorption peak appeared in the presence of TMB, H2O2 and Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt. However, the control experiments without catalysts or H2O2 showed a colorless and negligible absorbance change. As shown in Fig. 3D, Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt exhibited Michaelis–Menten catalytic kinetics in an appropriate substrate concentration range. The Km of Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt for H2O2 was calculated as 6.69 × 10−4 mol L−1, which reflects the high affinity of the nanozyme for H2O2 and is comparable to HRP (4.37 × 10−4 mol L−1) (Jiang et al., 2018). These results further confirmed Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt possessed superb POD-like ability and achieved excellent signal amplification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the stepwise fabricated dual-mode PAD
ce:section.ce:para: CV and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out to investigate the electrode interface properties during stepwise aptsensor fabrication (Zhang et al., 2020). In Fig. 3E, a reversible redox peak of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- appeared at bare WE. After WE modification with MnO2–Au, the peak current was significantly increased owing to the excellent conductivity and large surface area of MnO2–Au. Then, the peak currents decreased when apta1 was immobilized on the MnO2–Au/WE electrode via Au–S bond, which could be attributed to the fact that negatively charged phosphate group of apta1 repelled the [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and impeded the electron transfer of electrode interface (Kavosi et al., 2015). After the BSA blocked non-specific active sites on the sensing interface, the peak current further declined because BSA formed an insulating layer changed the steric hindrance (Chen et al., 2019). Finally, after the stepwise incubated with OTA and Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt-apta2, the peak current obviously decreased, indicating the formation of apta1-OTA-apta2 sandwich-type complex through specific recognition. The increased steric hindrance led to the inhibition of electron transfer rate between the electrode and the redox probe of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-.

EIS included a linear part representing for the diffusion limited process and a semicircle portion correlated with electron transfer-limited process (Wang et al., 2020). As shown in inset of Fig. 3F, the Randles equivalent circuit was used to model the EIS results, which consisted of four parameters, including the resistance of solution (Rs), electron-transfer resistance (Ret), the double layer capacitance (Cdl), and the Warburg impedance (Zw). The parameters of Randles equivalent circuit were simulated by ZSimWin software and the relevant data was shown in Table S1. As can be seen in Fig. 3F, the value of Ret of MnO2–Au/WE (315 Ω, curve b) was much smaller than that of bare WE due to the good electronic transfer capability of MnO2–Au. As the electrode was successively modified with apta1, BSA, OTA and Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt-apta2, Ret gradually increased to 878.1 Ω, indicating that these non-electroactive molecules were successfully immobilized. The good consistence between EIS and CV results confirmed the successful fabrication of the dual-mode PAD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility study of the dual-mode sensing system
ce:section.ce:para:
To verify the feasibility of sensing system, the electrochemical and colorimetric response was investigated by setting up different analysis conditions (Fig. S4). Due to the lack of Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt-apta2 or H2O2, no significant reduction current of H2O2 or color signal could be obtained (curve a and b) for that the bioconjugate of Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt-apta2/OTA cannot be formed, or no electroactive substrate was available. Then, a low signal at −0.44 V was observed owing to the nonspecific adsorption of Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt-apta2 (c). As expected, in the presence of OTA, Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt-apta2 and H
2
O
2 (d), the electroactive H
2
O
2 could be reduced on the electrode surface by the catalytic effect of the Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt. Then the coexistence of Mo4+/Mo6+ electron mediator further accelerated the electron transfer process, and a relatively large electrochemical response was detected. Meanwhile, the produced abundant ·OH can transfer outward to the visualization zone and then TMB was oxidized into blue derivative, resulting in high-intense colorimetric signal. Based on the above results, the proposed sensing platform could be implemented dual-mode OTA detection via independent signal outputs. And the possible sensing mechanism of aptsensor can be described as follows (3–5):
(3) H2O2+e− → ·OH
(4) Mo4+ ↔ Mo6++e−
(5) TMBred+·OH → TMBox(blue)+H2O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the dual-mode OTA PAD aptsensor
ce:section.ce:para: Meanwhile, a complementary visual detection could be implemented to prejudge if the OTA content in the sample beyond the standard, without the need of any external equipment. With the addition of the OTA sample, the ·OH is released and then TMB is oxidized to produce a blue derivative, enabling to a gray intensity enhancement of colorimetric area in Layer I. It was found that the color change was not clearly visible when the OTA concentration was below 100 pg mL−1. In Fig. 4B, the calibration plot presented a linear relationship between the ΔG (ΔG = G0-G, where G0 and G represent the gray intensity value of sensor incubated with 0 and a certain concentration of OTA, respectively) and the logarithmic values of the OTA concentrations ranged from 0.1 to 200 ng mL−1, where the linear equation was ΔG = 22.39lgC+51.14 (R2 = 0.9952). Benefiting from the dual-response signals combination of PAD, the detection accuracy and reliability can be improved to a certain extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the dual-mode PAD
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the complexity of the condition where designed PAD systems were employed in real-time analysis applications, AFB1, DON, ZEN, and T-2 as coexisting interfering mycotoxins in samples was used to evaluate the selectivity of the aptsensor (Fig. 4C). There was no significant disparity for OTA at 1 ng mL−1, compared to other mixed samples consisting of OTA and interfering species at a 100-fold higher concentration. The good selectivity could be attributed to the high specific recognition of the apta for OTA. Simultaneously, three different OTA levels (1 pg mL−1, 1 ng mL−1 and 100 ng mL−1, each level with 5 parallel prepared PAD) were used to evaluated the PAD reproducibility (Fig. 4D). The value of relative standard deviations (RSD) was 1.71, 1.27 and 1.38%, respectively, indicating that the electrochemical sensing platform has an acceptable reproducibility. Furthermore, the stability of PAD incubated with 1 ng mL−1 OTA was investigated. There was no significant difference (RSD = 1.34%) in the electrochemical signal after consecutive 10 scanning cycles (Fig. S6), demonstrating the high stability of the designed PAD.

To evaluate the practicability and accuracy of the developed dual-mode PAD, the standard addition method was implemented to monitor OTA in the uncontaminated corn sample from local supermarkets. For three spiked OTA concentrations (1, 5, and 100 ng mL−1), the average recovery ratio ranged from 94.8 to 106.0% with RSD below 4.10% (Table S2). These acceptable results indicated the good reliability, endowing the proposed device with potential applications for POCT.

In summary, an EC/colorimetric dual-mode aptasensor was successfully fabricated on the foldable PAD, which integrated with microfluidic channels, electrode modification, cleaning and dual-signal output components. Compared with previous reported methods, such a dual-mode PAD displayed highlights for OTA detection including the following aspects. (1) Based on wax printing and screen-printed methods, the microfluidic channel and three electrodes systems were fabricated, respectively, with merits of low-cost and mass production. (2) Taking advantage of the strategy of aptamer biorecognition and Ch-MoS2-Au@Pt signal amplification, the PAD provided a highly selective and sensitive sensing platform. (3) The integration of EC and colorimetric methods could give a mutual complementary and ensure a more reliable and accurate analysis result. Especially, the colorimetry was applicable for on-site detection of OTA without expensive apparatus and complicated operation. Overall, we expect that this current system may provide innovative inspiration for highly sensitive detection of OTA, as well expand to other food mycotoxins analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Key Project of CAS (Grant: KFJ-STS-ZDTP-083) and Dalian Youth Science and Technology Star Project (2019RQ071).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2021.113146.
Xiaobo Zhang: Conceptualization, ideas, data curation, writing original draft.
Hui Zhi: Formal analysis, review & editing.
Mingzhen Zhu: Visualization, investigation.
Fengya Wang: Formal analysis, verification.
Hu Meng: Methodology, funding acquisition.
Liang Feng: Funding acquisition, resources.
